speaker: interviewer
text: Bruce Cullen interviewing Halina Nelken, 24th of November 1995. Helena, just for clarification purposes, could you just please give the distinctions and dates between Birkenau and Auschwitz I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We arrived in Birkenau on the ramp because every transport to Auschwitz arrived in Birkenau on the ramp. And then, from Birkenau, where we were for two weeks, we went to Auschwitz I.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: About two weeks. After two weeks, we arrived in Auschwitz I. That was the central administration center for Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was about October of '44 you arrived in Birkenau. You're there two weeks. Then you went to Auschwitz, where you arrived possibly in October or November 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, we were staying until January 18, 1945 when Auschwitz was being evacuated. And then, for about two or maybe three weeks, we were on that transport in the cattle car through Gross-Rosen, Sachsenhausen, Oranienburg, till we arrived in Ravensbruck. And in Ravensbruck, for I don't know whether it was two weeks, or whatever, and then we were transported--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you had typhoid. At the time of the transport to-- and because of the typhoid, it's difficult for you to remember much about Ravensbruck. But is it possible that, even given the typhoid fever, you have any recollection of the atrocities that were taking place there? Directly, as an eyewitness.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that we were being packed into a barrack that was, perhaps, for 300 people, and we were over 1,000, and there was not even place to sit. And we were sitting somehow one inside another. And then, for a short time, we were in Polish barracks. There were many polish woman. And then, as a matter of fact, in Ravensbruck, we found out about the uprising in Warsaw. Because before, we did not know, in Auschwitz. And then, we were transported to Malchow.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in Ravensbruck, any evidence of medical experiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know anything about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any evidence of medical experiments that you were aware of in any of the other camps that we've talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were experiments in Auschwitz. There was the whole block.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke about Mengele, but you never took it further than that.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where-- well, thank God I saw him only during the selection. But there was the whole block where they were doing experiments. And later on, we sometimes saw the trucks taking people from this block, because this block was on the way when we were walking to our office.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know the medical experiments were taking place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because everybody knew that this is medical block.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd they know experimentation was taking place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody knew. This was not a secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: By what means?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not a secret. People know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were people chosen for those medical experiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were probably usually chosen during the selection. And they were mostly, for Mengele, was interested in twins. This is what I heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in any of the camps up until this point that we've mentioned, had you ever been aware of any instances of prostitution or rape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I know that there was in Auschwitz, there was a home for prostitutes. And even one of these prostitutes lived in the same block. And well, but they were German girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So we move now by transport from Ravensbruck to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then to Malchow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. And strangely enough, this was the first passenger train that we were riding with. And maybe because it was passenger train, I fared better.

speaker: interviewer
text: You actually had a seat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Beautiful seats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you still had typhoid at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had not, I don't know. But I was very, very weak.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you recover from typhoid? I mean, just purely by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Purely by myself. Well by, first of all, it was that the coffee, there was not, it was ersatz coffee there was what we were drinking. And they were such like coal. And this is what my friends feeded me with.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were friends that were helping you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my. Helping me with this. They were friends when there was-- well, I wouldn't be able even to go on this transport if not that they were so closely walking on both sides of me that I did not fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were my Polish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you name them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one is not alive anymore. Meska Geizhals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-E-I-Z-H-A-L-S. And this was my other friend, Rachel Knobler. R-A-C-H-E-L, Knobler, K-N-O-B-L-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to your eyewitness knowledge, did you ever see other instances of people helping people like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was frequent?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was maybe not so frequent, but it was. It took different forms, but it was. People who were friends--

speaker: interviewer
text: Other forms, examples of which would be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just talking, and helping, or keeping an arm around one another. Or really sharing. Bread, something. In Malchow, where we were not going anymore to work except these people who were in the kitchen working or something else, we were just simply starving in Malchow. Because we got first bread for five people, and then for six, and for eight, and for ten. So it means that it was such a piece of bread for 24 hours. And that soup, that water. It was one day at 6:00 in the morning, and another day at 10:00 in the evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were starved, and yet. Then, I remember when I was reading one poem to the room, ours, and I fainted away because I did not have energy. I woke up, I had a piece of bread in my mouth. Someone give it to me. And also, there were other girls who were singing, and who were well-- and really, noble price has no more worth than this little soup that was still left, that the whole room give to that people who were singing, or poets of this. Where was your mother and brother? Where did we leave them last, and where have they been?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was supposedly going to Gross-Rosen. And he went there, and then later on he went to Mauthausen. And from Mauthausen, he finally landed in Dachau. But I did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he split from you at what point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We split from them when we left Plaszow going to Auschwitz. But my mother was with me during the evacuation of Auschwitz. And on the first day, I noticed that said she is really-- for the first time, she said, I cannot go anymore. So I was desperately trying to help her. And since we were staying for about three, four hours in one of the villages there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there, and I begged the peasants if they could help my mother. Somehow, I did not have guts to tell them hid her, find hiding for her. Because I know this is death sentence for them, too, if she would. What do you mean peasants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were in a village, and we were in barn staying. The whole 62,000 people who were moving out of Auschwitz were in this barn a little bit, in that barn, in this-- you know, this was a march for miles and miles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute now. What date do we have here now?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 18th of January.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, wait a second. This is the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you asked me where I lost my mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is on the way to Ravensbruck then, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was 18 January was when we left Auschwitz. And after four days, my mother could not work anymore. So since we were stopping at the village, I noticed somewhere there a little door to something like a basement. And I give my mother bread, and blanket, and everything, and I let her go there. I wanted to stay with her too, but my sister-in-law didn't want it. And said, oh, I will tell everybody you are running, you are run-- OK. I left my mother, and I was hoping, since Russian were so close, that these people will keep her. And I went with my sister-in-law, and there we were going like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now to Ravensbruck, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. From Ravensbruck to Malchow. I was with her, with my sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you just said that with your mom, you were not on the way to Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. My mother stay in four days--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get back on the move to Ravensbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left my mother there in this, basement I locked this basement, and I joined the rest of Auschwitz girls moving to that train. This was before we were on the train. And then on this train, we went to Ravensbruck, and from Ravensbruck to Malchow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of your sister-in-law was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Genia. G-E-N-I-A. So I left with her. And then, in Malchow, when we were somehow maybe-- we did not real really go to work. But we exchanged a piece of bread with neighboring next to us civilian camp of Frenchmen. And they were civilian, and they had newspaper. And from their newspaper, we found out about the conference in Yalta. And so we were happy, because in two weeks, really, the war will be over. And we made there a revue. Half-starved as we were, we were singing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Malchow now, correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Malchow, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you engaged in any kind of work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I said that we did not, except this people who are working in the kitchen, or somewhere in-- we did not work, we were just our only work, it was to go to a roll call. But to go to roll call, it was terrible. Because at this time, we really were starved completely. And then, they send us down to Leipzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once again, on Malchow. Who was in charge of that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a woman who was once in Plaszow even. Her name was Dans, D-A-N-S. She was so terrible. After the war, she had a trial in Krakow, and I was witness against her. Because she was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Malchow located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mecklenburg, between Hamburg and Rostock. And then, we went through Magdeburg on the train, also open. And there were so many times that sometimes we thought, is this still that Auschwitz, or is this still Ravensbruck, or is this another transport? Because still cattle cars and open with the rain.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the trip from Malchow to Leipzig?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was about two and a half days. Because at one night, there was in Magdeburg terrible air raid. And we were the only train on the station, and there was flames all over there. But when we arrived in Leipzig, there was munition factory. And they had very good food. They had bread not for ten, but for three people, or two people, and very good soup. And during this one week we were there in Leipzig, we somehow improve so that we were able to go for this march, which was for about two weeks, or three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they were preparing you for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody was prepared.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason for the better treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because everything was depending on the commandant. The commandant of this lager was an officer who knew that munition factory, and workers in munition factories always get much more than anybody else. And he was still stealing, because everybody was stealing. But still, it was enough for the other people who were there to get good food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, because I was there only for a week. But also, there were also in this camp, there were Russian woman who were Russian soldiers. And they were actually POWs, and they were not supposed to be there. But they didn't want to go to work when they took them, so they let them stand for 24 hours on the roll call without food, without anything. And so they were standing. And then, the German woman came and slapped one of them. And then the Russian right away give back to her, slapped the German woman, too. And starts yelling, and said, we are not going to the munition factory. We are not going to work. We won't do anything to work against people who are soldiers. So anyway, they went to the kitchen. And when they were in the kitchen, the food was fine. So this is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that they sent us all out, and nobody knew what they are doing with us. On one side were American, on another side were Russian, and we were walking and walking in circles between Leipzig, and Dresden, and Leipzig, and Dresden. And we did not get anything to eat. We were just eating grass.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you going back and forth in circles between these two places?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was still about the first of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's do Malchow for a moment. You arrived in Malchow on what date, then, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think maybe in February

speaker: interviewer
text: And you left there in--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was March, and maybe in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of 1945. And so you arrived, then, in Leipzig probably--

speaker: interviewee
text: In middle of April. Maybe beginning of April. First week of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were there only for about approximately a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then at that point, which was now April, possibly early May, this march back and forth between out of Leipzig took place, and Dresden.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about the 1st of May, I finally decide I cannot work anymore. And I just went to the ditch, and just sit there. And then later, Genia sit there with me, and then they come. The whole transport passed by us, and we were finally all free all by ourselves. And this was how we were free, more or less. But then--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were free, but essentially you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were free, and we were not free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we just, next day we just dig our striped jackets somewhere else, and we decide to go. But we had underneath something else. And we went to the village, and we wanted to say that we were civilian workers, and that we lost our things, everything, during that air raid, whatever. And the first person that we met, and I wanted to say this in beautiful German, he told me, I don't understand, I'm Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was a civilian Polish worker, and he took us to other Poles there. And there, we did not, of course, said that we are from that transport, because there was already such a mess of everybody-- civilians, soldiers, this, that, everything was working in different direction. So our location here now is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Nossen, by Dresden. Not far away from Dresden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date now is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about the 1st or 2nd of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So if you were to have been liberated by anyone, essentially who would that have been? Who was that? Who would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I still was not liberated. Well, then, at this time, they told us that the German peasants, they wanted very much to have foreigners because they need to work. Everybody ran away. And anyway, so I went to a peasant like this, and he was the first home out of this village. And then, an hour later, there were German soldiers, and they decide to-- well, there was a battle. The last battle during the Second World War, it was probably there where I was. And the German took me down to the basement, the peasant, and I was sitting there with them. And I said, what an irony. Here, if something will, well, bang at this house, I will be dead with two Germans, and nobody will know about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But nothing was happening, except that suddenly the German run away, the soldiers who were there. And we hear steps, and some [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] And I knew that no German speaks that way. So I banged up, and there was a very tired Russian soldier. And I jump on him, and embrace him, and I start crying, and he was afraid of me. He said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Was, what do you want, you German woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, no, I am not-- and I show him this, and he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], I know. And then, at this moment, he said, Auschwitz, and I knew that he knew. But I was with this German peasant for about three days or four days while I was working for them. Well, taking care of cows, and this, and that, and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And anyway, he just asked, were they good? If they were not good, I will kill him. And I was just looking at them, and I know that under my bed there was a suitcase. And in this suitcase was a Gestapo uniform. And this was room of their son. And, you know, this is strange. Because it takes about half a minute. Everything in my mind. Why do I care about them? Perhaps their son was killing my father, or beating, or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: But on the other hand, when I was looking at them, how frightened they were, then I know that they are frightened. This is the expression that I know from all of us. And then I said after all, to me they were good. But my [NON-ENGLISH WORD], my goddess of victory, it was that Russian soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name by chance?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Some kind vanya, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was probably what day? That was now--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this was the 8th of May. Just almost the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody give you food and clothes at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working with the peasant woman, so I get the food, and she gave me clothes because I had everything torn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mom now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know either. So together with Genia, our we went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Home to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Krakow. To what location?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went home, to my own home. And there, I came to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], to our home. And they were our not to our apartment, but to our home, where they were still the renters, from the [NON-ENGLISH] who knew me, who knew my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people who rented from your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, who are living in our home for generations. And really, that hour--

speaker: interviewer
text: But not on your floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in your floor? Who was on the second--

speaker: interviewee
text: Strange people. They did not know them, I did not know. But I want to say that our superintendent, or janitor, he was sleeping on the floor, and he gave me his bed. And his wife was sleeping with the children. And I was sleeping in his bed, he was sleeping on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the renters who let you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was staying, the next day, I went to the Jewish committee. And to that association for former, well, prisoners of concentration camp. And then, I was trying to find out my mother. But there, when I went there, when I left her, I found out she is not there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you-- oh, please, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did not know, really. I came go back to Krakow completely crushed. And I did not know what to do. And when I was so terribly depressed, then someone grabbed me on the street, and says, your mother is crying her eyes out in Mauthausen because she does not know where you are. So this is how I found out that my mother is alive. And my mother came in about two weeks to Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still-- did you know where your brother was at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not know anything about my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then were you married at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, come on.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, at what point did you become married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Late, in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what location?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married a man. And well, he was not Jewish, and he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edward Morawski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edward, E-D-W-A-R-D, Morawski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a child with that man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish name is Leszek, L-E-S-Z-E-K. American name is Les. Les Daniel Nelken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born on January 7, 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married on what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was divorced in September, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came to the United States when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to United States in 1959.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop here.
